body:
## Summary

Finished isolation-task containers (the `$ --isolated docker --detached --session <uuid>` runs spawned per task) are **never removed**. After a task completes, the bot sends its Telegram notification and calls `completeSession()`, which only drops in-memory/durable tracking — there is **no `docker rm`**. The containers linger as `Exited`, each holding a multi-GB writable layer (Rust `target/debug`, `node_modules/onnxruntime`+CUDA, browser binaries, cloned repos). They accumulate until something external prunes them.

**Observed:** on one deployment, finished/leftover isolation containers held **~35 GB** of writable layers; the host filled to ~89%. Manually removing exited task containers + old images reclaimed ~22 GB and dropped usage to 66%.

## Proposed behavior

When an isolated task reaches a **terminal** state, remove its container filesystem (not just leave it `Exited`) so CPU/RAM/disk is reclaimed — while keeping the log, which already lives on the host.

Hive Mind is in a good position to make this **success/failure-aware**, because it already detects the terminal exit code:

- **Task succeeds → `docker rm -f <sessionId>`** (remove the container fs). Nothing in it is worth keeping.
- **Task fails → keep the container fs** for investigation, and surface the cleanup command (e.g. in the Telegram completion message): `docker rm -f <sessionId>`.

This mirrors the proposed default in the upstream `$` command (`link-foundation/start`) — see companion issue linked below — but Hive Mind can act on its own terminal-status detection regardless of what `$` defaults to.

### The log is safe to keep when removing the container

The task log is written by `$`/start-command on the **host** filesystem (referenced as `statusResult.logPath`), not inside the container's writable layer. So `docker rm <sessionId>` after completion does **not** lose the log. (`src/log-upload.lib.mjs` can still upload it as a gist.)

## Current code (as of `main`)

- **Spawn:** `src/isolation-runner.lib.mjs`
  - `buildDockerIsolationStartArgs()` — lines **213–239**: builds `['--isolated','docker','--image',image, …, '--detached','--session',sessionId,'--',<cmd>]`. **No removal flag** is passed to `$`, and `$`'s detached docker path preserves the container by default.
  - Container is named after the session UUID — confirmed by `checkDockerContainerRunning()` doing `docker inspect -f {{.State.Running}} <containerName>` (lines **820–832**).
  - `executeWithIsolation()` — lines **546–623**; `runStartCommand()` — lines **249–285**.
- **Completion path (no cleanup):** `src/session-monitor.lib.mjs`
  - `monitorSessions()` completion branch — lines **602–775**; on `!stillRunning` it computes the exit code, sends/edits the Telegram message, then calls `completeSession(...)` at line **761**.
  - `completeSession()` — lines **256–272**: only `activeSessions.delete(...)` + drop from `sessionStore` + emit `session_completed`. **No `docker rm` / `docker container prune`.**
- **Exit status is already known:** `getSessionCompletionExitCode()` / `classifySessionOutcome()`, plus log-footer parsing `readSessionExitFromLog()` / `parseSessionExitFooter()` in `isolation-runner.lib.mjs` lines **430–483**. So success-vs-failure-aware cleanup needs no new detection.
- **Existing cleanup is host-wide, not per-task:** `src/cleanup.mjs` (`--docker` → `docker system prune -f`) is a blunt manual tool, not keyed to finished task containers.

## Suggested implementation

In `session-monitor.lib.mjs`, right after `completeSession(...)` (line 761) for a terminally-finished session:

```js
// task container is named after the session UUID
if (isolationBackendWasDocker && finalExitCode === 0) {
  await run(`docker rm -f ${sessionName}`, { ignoreErrors: true }); // success: reclaim fs
} else if (isolationBackendWasDocker) {
  // failure: keep for investigation, tell the user how to inspect & clean up
  appendToCompletionMessage(
    `Container kept for debugging: ${sessionName}\n` +
    `Inspect: docker start -ai ${sessionName}  (or docker exec -it ${sessionName} sh)\n` +
    `Remove when done: docker rm -f ${sessionName}`
  );
}
```

Make the policy configurable (env or flag), e.g. `HIVE_MIND_KEEP_TASK_CONTAINER=always|on-failure|never` (default `on-failure`).

Guards:
- Only remove when the session is truly terminal (respect the existing `isUnknownDockerExitCode` / sentinel `-1` ambiguity guard, `isolation-runner.lib.mjs:401-403`) — never remove a still-running container.
- Best-effort (`ignoreErrors`): a missing/already-removed container must not break completion handling.
- Confirm the log has been read/persisted (PR-URL extraction etc. already read `logPath` before this point) before removing.

## Existing issues

A scan found **no** existing issue tracking removal of finished isolation-task containers. Related but different: #1914 (`--isolation docker` image-pull/disk), #1962 (DooD vs DinD), #1945 (disk diagnostics in /solve, closed), #336/#912/#1848 (host-level cleanup). This is an unfiled gap.

## Related

- Upstream companion issue in `link-foundation/start`: make "remove container fs when the command finishes" the default for `$ --isolated docker` (keeping the log; opt-out `--keep-container`; optional success/failure awareness). Hive Mind's per-task reaping complements that and can act on its own exit-status detection.

---
**Upstream companion issue:** link-foundation/start#140 (make container removal the default for `$ --isolated docker`).

comments.body:
Companion issue: #1980 — the manual/on-demand counterpart. This issue (#1979) auto-reaps each container at task completion (event-driven, in `session-monitor.lib.mjs`); #1980 makes the `hive-cleanup` command itself docker-isolation-aware so it can selectively sweep finished task containers on demand (today its `--docker` flag only does a blunt host-wide `docker system prune -f`, and its session enumeration is screen/tmux-only — docker-blind). Complementary, not duplicate.


We also need to make sure we use latest version of start command in our docker image. As https://github.com/link-foundation/start/issues/140 now fixed.

We need to download all logs and data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), in which we will reconstruct timeline/sequence of events, list of each and all requirements from the issue, find root causes of the each problem, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

If there is not enough data to find actual root cause, add debug output and verbose mode if not present, that will allow us to find root cause on next iteration.

If issue related to any other repository/project, where we can report issues on GitHub, please do so. Each issue must contain reproducible examples, workarounds and suggestions for fix the issue in code. Also double check to fully apply requirements to entire codebase, so if we have issue in multiple places, it should be fixed in all them.

Please plan and execute everything in this single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.

title: Reap finished isolation-task containers: docker rm on success, keep on failure (free the multi-GB writable layers)
